zotero.abstractNote: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly fine-tuned on domain-specific data to improve their performance on downstream tasks. While this process is generally considered benign, we demonstrate that it can inadvertently lead to a 'privacy collapse,' where the model's ability to maintain contextual privacy—the principle that information should only be disclosed in appropriate contexts—is severely compromised. We show that even fine-tuning on seemingly innocuous, non-sensitive data can cause the model to leak private information that was present in its pre-training data but was previously protected by the model's contextual awareness. We analyze this phenomenon through the lens of representation learning and show that fine-tuning can distort the model's internal representations, making it easier to extract sensitive information. We propose a mitigation strategy based on differential privacy and demonstrate its effectiveness in preventing privacy collapse.
